papers.general_reader_takeaway: This paper attempts to show that a proposed theory of quantum gravity, built from fractional (non-integer power) versions of the wave operator, can be made mathematically consistent in the sense that probabilities are conserved across particle interactions. The review board found the tree-level spectral analysis — a detailed classification of which modes appear in the propagator and on which mathematical sheets — to be a genuine and useful technical contribution. However, the central claim of unitarity at *all* perturbative orders is extrapolated from one-loop examples without a rigorous inductive proof, and the extension to gauge and gravitational theories omits the BRST/ghost framework that such proofs require.
papers.technical_takeaway: The paper performs a systematic Riemann-surface analysis of the tree-level propagator for self-adjoint fractional d'Alembertians, classifying poles across four kinetic-term variants (HP, HS, HA, NH) and proving that pole-free Riemann sheets exist in the super-renormalizable parameter range (Theorem 2). The physical spectrum is argued to consist of a single standard massless/massive mode, with all complex-conjugate pairs and continuum modes relegated to a purely virtual sector via the Anselmi–Piva fakeon prescription. Unitarity at all perturbative orders is claimed but demonstrated explicitly only at one loop for scalar toy models and schematic derivative-vertex models; the gauge/gravity extension suppresses BRST/ghost structure and the continuum-fakeon step requires commuting mass integration with average analytic continuation under conditions that are stated but not verified.
papers.title: Perturbative unitarity of fractional field theories and gravity
